scenarios.title: Claim for pre-existing battery issue after delayed AppleCare+ purchase
scenarios.description: Customer purchases AppleCare+ six months after buying their iPhone and immediately files a claim for a battery that has already degraded below 50% capacity.

service_fee.notes: Battery service is normally covered without a fee, but pre-existing conditions are excluded.

policy_references.snippet: If during the Plan Term, you submit a valid claim ... the capacity of the Covered iPhone’s battery to hold an electrical charge has depleted fifty (50%) percent or more ... Apple will ... repair ... or ... exchange the Covered iPhone.
The Plan does not apply to a pre-existing condition on any Covered iPhone if you purchased the Plan after you purchased the Covered iPhone.

scenarios.reasoning: While battery degradation below 50% is normally covered under section 3.1, section 4.2(viii) explicitly excludes pre-existing conditions if AppleCare+ is purchased after the device. The policy does not define how to determine if a battery issue is pre-existing at the time of plan purchase, creating an edge case. Apple would need to verify battery health history or usage data to adjudicate the claim.

scenarios.title: Claim for ADH after device repaired by non-authorized provider
scenarios.description: Customer seeks AppleCare+ accidental damage coverage after having a cracked screen repaired by a non-Apple provider, then experiencing touchscreen malfunction.

service_fee.notes: Fee applies only if claim is accepted as valid ADH despite prior third-party repair.

policy_references.snippet: If during the Plan Term you submit a valid claim notifying Apple that the Covered iPhone has failed due to accidental damage from handling ('ADH'), Apple will ... repair or exchange the Covered iPhone.
The Plan does not apply to service (including upgrades and expansions) if such was performed by anyone who is not a representative of Apple or an Apple Authorized Service Provider ('AASP').

scenarios.reasoning: The claim is for accidental damage, which is generally covered (3.2), but the device was previously serviced by a non-authorized provider, which is excluded (4.2(ii)(d)). The malfunction may be due to the third-party repair or to the original accident. Determining eligibility requires judgment on whether the current failure is directly caused by the unauthorized repair or is a new, unrelated ADH event. Apple would need to assess the device and possibly request documentation of both the accident and the repair.
